summary:
The Carolingian Empire under Charlemagne (r. 768–814 AD) was the first attempt to reunite western Europe under a single Christian ruler since the fall of Rome — an achievement that gave rise to the concept of 'Christendom' as a political as well as religious community. Charlemagne (Charles the Great), king of the Franks, conducted over 50 military campaigns to extend Christian Frankish rule over Saxony (forcibly converting the Saxons), Bavaria, northern Spain, and Italy, creating an empire that encompassed most of western and central Europe. His [[event:2139|coronation as Emperor]] by Pope Leo III on Christmas Day 800 AD was the founding act of what would become the Holy Roman Empire.

Charlemagne's court at Aachen promoted the [[event:2140|Carolingian Renaissance]] — a deliberate revival of Latin learning, manuscript copying, standardized script (Carolingian minuscule, ancestor of modern lowercase letters), church reform, and education. His administrative innovations — including the missi dominici (royal inspectors) — attempted to govern a vast, pre-modern empire through loyal representatives. After Charlemagne's death his empire was divided among his grandsons by the [[event:2141|Treaty of Verdun]] (843 AD), creating the political outlines of modern France, Germany, and the Netherlands. Despite its fragmentation, the Carolingian Empire left an enduring legacy: the concept of a unified Christian Europe, the Carolingian administrative and educational traditions, and the ideal of a Christian emperor that would drive European politics for centuries.
